narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated an 84% chance that you would not pass based on your academic profile. This high predicted risk of failure triggered the rejection to help the university maintain strong bar passage rates for its graduates. The model's decision hinged most strongly on your ranking in Year 3 grades, where you placed in the bottom decile of your school—far below the top-half average seen among students who went on to pass the bar. This low Year 3 performance was the biggest factor pushing the prediction toward failure, as it signals potential challenges in sustaining the rigorous demands of legal studies and bar preparation. Next in influence was your Year 1 grade decile, also in the lower ranks compared to the mid-to-high deciles typical of bar passers, which added further weight toward the failure outlook. Your LSAT score of 30 likewise pulled strongly in the same direction, sitting well under the 37 average for those who succeed on the bar, reinforcing concerns about foundational skills. While your undergraduate GPA of 3.20 was close to the 3.24 average of successful students and your lower-middle family income aligns with a smaller but present group of bar passers, these did not offset the top concerns. Being a full-time student is common among those who pass, so that worked slightly in your favor, but not enough to shift the overall picture. In essence, your profile stood out as riskier than the typical accepted applicant, whose stronger decile rankings and higher LSAT scores predict a much lower failure probability. To move forward, focus on retaking the LSAT to aim for at least 37 or higher, while building a stronger academic record through additional coursework or a post-baccalaureate program that boosts your decile standings. Consider targeted bar prep resources early, and reapply once you've addressed these key gaps—many students in similar situations have succeeded by showing clear improvement in these areas. Your determination as a full-time aspiring lawyer from a modest background can still pave the way; persistence here makes all the difference.
